sources.citation: Suetonius, Life of Julius Caesar 37.2 (Veni, vidi, vici dispatch)
Plutarch, Life of Caesar (Tusculum-type bust tradition; senatorial portrait)
British Museum — Roman Republican denarius portraits of Caesar (partial_visual)

beats.speech_text: Tonight: Gaius Julius Caesar — general, dictator, and the man who made Rome an empire in all but name.
Born into a patrician family in 100 BCE, he climbed from quaestor to consul, conquered Gaul in a decade, and crossed the Rubicon with a civil war in his wake.
His world was a Republic in name only — senators armed, provinces plundered, and the calendar itself still drifting until he fixed the year.
They killed him on the Ides of March — but his name became the title of emperors, and his reforms outlived the blades that struck him down.
